story: A revival of the Classical style in architecture was accompanied by a burgeoning of science and engineering which affected the proportions and structure of buildings. At this stage, it was still possible for an artist to design a bridge as the level of structural calculations involved was within the scope of the generalist.
q1: The Classical revival in architecture had no connection to advances in science and engineering. (true/false)
q2: Structural calculations for bridge design were too complex for a generalist to handle at this stage. (true/false)

story: One such reaction to the cold aesthetic of modernism and Brutalism is the school of metaphoric architecture, which includes such things as biomorphism and zoomorphic architecture, both using nature as the primary source of inspiration and design. While it is considered by some to be merely an aspect of postmodernism, others consider it to be a school in its own right and a later development of expressionist architecture.
q1: Metaphoric architecture draws its primary inspiration from urban design rather than nature. (true/false)
q2: Biomorphism and zoomorphic architecture are examples of metaphoric architecture. (true/false)

story: Concurrently, the recent movements of New Urbanism, Metaphoric architecture and New Classical Architecture promote a sustainable approach towards construction, that appreciates and develops smart growth, architectural tradition and classical design. This in contrast to modernist and globally uniform architecture, as well as leaning against solitary housing estates and suburban sprawl.
q1: New Urbanism, Metaphoric architecture and New Classical Architecture support suburban sprawl and solitary housing estates. (true/false)
q2: These movements promote smart growth and classical design as part of sustainable construction. (true/false)

story: The 2010 Human Development Report introduced an Inequality-adjusted Human Development Index (IHDI). While the simple HDI remains useful, it stated that "the IHDI is the actual level of human development (accounting for inequality)," and "the HDI can be viewed as an index of 'potential' human development (or the maximum IHDI that could be achieved if there were no inequality)."
q1: The IHDI accounts for inequality while the HDI represents potential human development. (true/false)
q2: The IHDI was abandoned in favor of the simpler HDI measure. (true/false)

story: The 2009 Human Development Report by UNDP was released on October 5, 2009, and covers the period up to 2007. It was titled "Overcoming barriers: Human mobility and development". The top countries by HDI were grouped in a new category called "very high human development". The report refers to these countries as developed countries. They are:
q1: The 2009 Human Development Report was released on October 5, 2009. (true/false)
q2: The report covered countries up to the year 2008. (true/false)

story: A new index was released on December 18, 2008. This so-called "statistical update" covered the period up to 2006 and was published without an accompanying Human Development Report. The update is relevant due to newly released estimates of purchasing power parities (PPP), implying substantial adjustments for many countries, resulting in changes in HDI values and, in many cases, HDI ranks.
q1: The statistical update released in December 2008 included an accompanying Human Development Report. (true/false)
q2: The update involved newly released estimates of purchasing power parities. (true/false)

story: The report showed a small increase in world HDI in comparison with last year's report. This rise was fueled by a general improvement in the developing world, especially of the least developed countries group. This marked improvement at the bottom was offset with a decrease in HDI of high income countries.
q1: High income countries experienced a decrease in HDI. (true/false)
q2: The least developed countries did not contribute to the global increase in HDI. (true/false)

story: A HDI of 0.8 or more is considered to represent "high development". This includes all developed countries, such as those in North America, Western Europe, Oceania, and Eastern Asia, as well as some developing countries in Eastern Europe, Central and South America, Southeast Asia, the Caribbean, and the oil-rich Arabian Peninsula. Seven countries were promoted to this category this year, leaving the "medium development" group: Albania, Belarus, Brazil, Libya, Macedonia, Russia and Saudi Arabia.
q1: Seven countries were promoted from the medium development group to high development this year. (true/false)
q2: A HDI below 0.8 represents high development. (true/false)

story: On the following table, green arrows () represent an increase in ranking over the previous study, while red arrows () represent a decrease in ranking. They are followed by the number of spaces they moved. Blue dashes () represent a nation that did not move in the rankings since the previous study.
q1: Red arrows indicate an increase in ranking. (true/false)
q2: Green arrows show that a country moved up in the rankings. (true/false)

story: The HDI has extended its geographical coverage: David Hastings, of the United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific, published a report geographically extending the HDI to 230+ economies, whereas the UNDP HDI for 2009 enumerates 182 economies and coverage for the 2010 HDI dropped to 169 countries.
q1: The 2010 HDI coverage increased to 230 economies. (true/false)
q2: David Hastings extended the HDI to over 230 economies. (true/false)

story: Some definitions of southern Europe, also known as Mediterranean Europe, include the countries of the Iberian peninsula (Spain and Portugal), the Italian peninsula, southern France and Greece. Other definitions sometimes include the Balkan countries of southeast Europe, which are geographically in the southern part of Europe, but which have different historical, political, economic, and cultural backgrounds.
q1: Southern Europe is also known as Mediterranean Europe. (true/false)
q2: All definitions of southern Europe exclude the Balkan countries. (true/false)

story: Southern Europe's most emblematic climate is that of the Mediterranean climate, which has become a typically known characteristic of the area. The Mediterranean climate covers much of Portugal, Spain, Southeast France, Italy, Croatia, Albania, Montenegro, Greece, the Western and Southern coastal regions of Turkey as well as the Mediterranean islands. Those areas of Mediterranean climate present similar vegetations and landscapes throughout, including dry hills, small plains, pine forests and olive trees.
q1: The Mediterranean climate in southern Europe is characterized by similar vegetations and landscapes. (true/false)
q2: Inland regions of Turkey have the Mediterranean climate. (true/false)

story: Southern Europe's flora is that of the Mediterranean Region, one of the phytochoria recognized by Armen Takhtajan. The Mediterranean and Submediterranean climate regions in Europe are found in much of Southern Europe, mainly in Southern Portugal, most of Spain, the southern coast of France, Italy, the Croatian coast, much of Bosnia, Montenegro, Albania, Macedonia, Greece, and the Mediterranean islands.
q1: Armen Takhtajan recognized the Mediterranean Region as a phytochoria. (true/false)
q2: The Submediterranean climate is not found in southern Europe. (true/false)

story: The period between 1815 and 1871 saw a large number of revolutionary attempts and independence wars. Balkan nations began to regain independence from the Ottoman Empire. Italy unified into a nation state. The capture of Rome in 1870 ended the Papal temporal power. Rivalry in a scramble for empires spread in what is known as The Age of Empire.
q1: The capture of Rome in 1870 ended Papal temporal power. (true/false)
q2: Italy remained fragmented during the period 1815-1871. (true/false)

story: The outbreak of World War I in 1914 was precipitated by the rise of nationalism in Southeastern Europe as the Great Powers took up sides. The Allies defeated the Central Powers in 1918. During the Paris Peace Conference the Big Four imposed their terms in a series of treaties, especially the Treaty of Versailles.
q1: World War I began in 1914. (true/false)
q2: The Central Powers were defeated before 1918. (true/false)

story: For its official works and publications, the United Nations Organization groups countries under a classification of regions. The assignment of countries or areas to specific groupings is for statistical convenience and does not imply any assumption regarding political or other affiliation of countries or territories by the United Nations. Southern Europe, as grouped for statistical convenience by the United Nations (the sub-regions according to the UN), includes following countries and territories:
q1: The UN groups countries into regions for statistical convenience. (true/false)
q2: The UN's regional groupings imply political affiliation of countries. (true/false)

story: BBC Television is a service of the British Broadcasting Corporation. The corporation, which has operated in the United Kingdom under the terms of a Royal charter since 1927, has produced television programmes from its own since 1932, although the start of its regular service of television broadcasts is dated to 2 November 1936.
q1: The BBC began regular television broadcasts on 2 November 1936. (true/false)
q2: The BBC has held a Royal charter since 1925. (true/false)

story: The BBC operates several television networks, television stations (although there is generally very little distinction between the two terms in the UK), and related programming services in the United Kingdom. As well as being a broadcaster, the corporation also produces a large number of its own programmes in-house, thereby ranking as one of the world's largest television production companies.
q1: The BBC is one of the world's largest television production companies. (true/false)
q2: The BBC relies entirely on external production companies for its programming. (true/false)

story: The BBC began its own regular television programming from the basement of Broadcasting House, London, on 22 August 1932. The studio moved to larger quarters in 16 Portland Place, London, in February 1934, and continued broadcasting the 30-line images, carried by telephone line to the medium wave transmitter at Brookmans Park, until 11 September 1935, by which time advances in all-electronic television systems made the electromechanical broadcasts obsolete.
q1: The BBC's first studio was located at Broadcasting House basement. (true/false)
q2: The BBC moved to 16 Portland Place in March 1934. (true/false)

story: The two systems were to run on a trial basis for six months; early television sets supported both resolutions. However, the Baird system, which used a mechanical camera for filmed programming and Farnsworth image dissector cameras for live programming, proved too cumbersome and visually inferior, and ended with closedown (at 22:00) on Saturday 13 February 1937.
q1: The Baird system was abandoned on 13 February 1937. (true/false)
q2: The Farnsworth image dissector was used for filmed programming in the Baird system. (true/false)
